Ablation results show that supervised fine-tuning provides the largest gain, while synthetic-speech LoRA adaptation and reinforcement learning further improve robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a system for multilingual conversational speech recognition that combines advanced speaker diarization with a fine-tuned ASR model.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges in multilingual two-speaker conversational speech diarization and recognition under the MLC-SLM 2026 Challenge framework.
AI.method: The system features a speaker diarization front end that groups segments by speaker and language, paired with a Qwen3-ASR-1.7B recognizer fine-tuned using supervised learning, synthetic data augmentation, and reinforcement learning.
AI.result: The proposed system outperforms baseline models with a 23.70 tcpMER on the development set (6.83-point improvement) and a 17.97 tcpMER on the evaluation set.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates how combining speaker diarization with iterative ASR adaptations can significantly enhance multilingual conversational speech processing.

title: Dual-Correlation Hypergraph Network for Unaligned RGBT Video Object Detection and A Large-scale Benchmark
summary: RGB-Thermal (RGBT) Video Object Detection (VOD) has gained significant traction due to its ability to overcome the limitations of conventional RGB-based VOD under challenging conditions. However, spatial misalignment commonly exists between RGBT image pairs. To address this, we propose a Dual-Correlation Hypergraph Network (DHNet) that captures high-dimensional complementary information by explicitly modeling two types of correlations: temporal correlation across consecutive frames and spatial correlation from cross-modal features. Specifically, we first design a Patch-based Spatial Alignment Module (PSAM) to sequentially align the multimodal features at the local region level. Subsequently, we introduce a Dual Hypergraph Fusion Module (DHFM), which constructs separate temporal and multimodal hypergraphs to enhance object discriminability through dual-correlation learning. Furthermore, the field currently lacks a large-scale, scene-diverse benchmark dataset for comprehensive evaluation. To address this gap, we construct DVT-VOD1000, a large-scale RGBT VOD dataset containing 1,000 video sequences with 103,464 RGBT image pairs. The dataset covers diverse scenarios, including campuses, parks, transportation, rural areas, night scenes, rain, and snow. Comprehensive experiments on VT-VOD50 and our DVT-VOD1000 demonstrate that DHNet achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy. The dataset and source code will be made publicly available on https://github.com/tzz-ahu/ to support academic research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Dual-Correlation Hypergraph Network (DHNet) for RGB-Thermal Video Object Detection (VOD), addressing spatial misalignment in RGB and thermal image pairs. It also presents a new large-scale dataset, DVT-VOD1000.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of RGB-based video object detection under challenging conditions and address the spatial misalignment issue in RGB-Thermal image pairs.
AI.method: The authors propose DHNet, which utilizes a Patch-based Spatial Alignment Module (PSAM) for multimodal feature alignment and a Dual Hypergraph Fusion Module (DHFM) to enhance object discriminability by dual-correlation learning across temporal and multimodal domains.
AI.result: The proposed approach, DHNet, achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy on existing dataset VT-VOD50 and a newly introduced dataset, DVT-VOD1000.
AI.conclusion: DHNet effectively addresses spatial misalignment in RGBT VOD and provides significant detection improvements. The new DVT-VOD1000 dataset supports further research and development in the field.

title: ASMR: Agentic Schema Generation for Ship Maintenance Report Writing
comment: Accepted at the DASHSys 2026 workshop (Systems for Data-centric Agents with Human-in-the-loop), co-located with VLDB 2026
summary: In this paper, we study the automatic schema generation problem: given a collection of historical ship maintenance and operational reports across multiple form categories, automatically discover compact and informative schemas that capture the essential information requirements of each report type. To address this challenge, we propose ASMR, a modular agentic framework consisting of two specialized agents. A Field Generation Agent extracts semantic concepts from historical narratives and generates candidate schema fields through adaptive multi-granularity clustering, while a Structural Optimizer Agent employs reinforcement learning to identify compact, informative, and non-redundant schema representations. The resulting schemas can guide report authors toward producing more complete, consistent, and actionable reports. Preliminary results demonstrate the promise of the proposed approach and highlight several open research challenges at the intersection of data management, agentic AI, and human-centered AI.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ASMR, a framework to automatically generate compact and informative schemas for ship maintenance reports using two AI agents.
AI.motivation: To create efficient and structured schemas from large collections of historical ship maintenance and operational reports to improve report consistency and usability.
AI.method: The ASMR framework includes two agents: a Field Generation Agent for extracting semantic concepts and clustering them, and a Structural Optimizer Agent using reinforcement learning to refine and optimize schema representations.
AI.result: Preliminary results indicate the promise of the ASMR framework and its utility in guiding report authors, though several challenges remain.
AI.conclusion: ASMR provides a novel solution to schema generation, leveraging agentic AI techniques, and opens avenues for further research in data management and human-centered AI.

title: Physics-Informed Machine Learning Under Small-Data Constraints: Lessons from Abrasive Waterjet Milling
comment: 2 figures, 5 tables, Accepted for publication; to be published in the Proceedings of ICANN 2026, Springer LNCS
summary: In physically dominated machining processes, experimental datasets are small, expensive, and material-specific; in this regime, data curation, evaluation design, and the form of physics integration can matter as much as the learning algorithm. Using an abrasive waterjet milling dataset ($n{=}155$, Inconel\,718), we make three methodological contributions. First, we separate physics-based data \emph{cleaning} from statistical \emph{curation} and treat the latter as competing modelling hypotheses rather than silent preprocessing. Second, we find that model rankings from a 15-point hold-out set can be unstable: the single-split winner drops from rank~1 to rank~7 under 10-fold cross-validation, while Gaussian Process (GP) variants occupy the top ranks. Third, we study a spectrum of physics integration levels and find that residual learning on a compact physics baseline is competitive for GP, yielding lower variance and an interpretable decomposition, but degrades tree-based models. Bayesian hyper parameter tuning improves parameter-sensitive baselines such as gradient boosting and SVR, yet harms multi-stage hybrid pipelines at this sample size. GP uncertainty intervals are approximately calibrated ($86\%$ empirical coverage at nominal $90\%$). The resulting picture is methodological: for small, expensive process datasets, our results suggest that, in this setting, reliable model comparison benefits from explicit curation hypotheses, robust evaluation, and careful choices about how physics enters the model.

AI.tldr: The study explores modeling strategies for small, expensive datasets in physics-dominated machining processes (abrasive waterjet milling). It emphasizes curating data as modeling hypotheses, instability in model rankings, and the effectiveness of residual learning while highlighting nuances in physics integration and model evaluation.
AI.motivation: Small datasets in physics-dominated machining processes are expensive and material-specific, requiring meticulous attention to data curation, evaluation, and the integration of physics into learning algorithms.
AI.method: The paper analyzes an abrasive waterjet milling dataset (n=155, Inconel 718) with three key approaches: separating physics-based cleaning from statistical curation; comparing model rankings via single-split vs. cross-validation; and examining physics integration levels and their impact on model types.
AI.result: Gaussian Process (GP) models perform consistently well, residual learning aids compact physics baselines but not tree-based models, Bayesian tuning benefits parameter-sensitive models, and GP uncertainty intervals are well-calibrated.
AI.conclusion: For small, expensive datasets, explicit curation hypotheses, robust evaluation methods, and deliberate physics-integration strategies are essential for reliable model performance comparison and improved dataset analysis.

title: DexVerse: A Modular Benchmark for Multi-Task, Multi-Embodiment Dexterous Manipulation
summary: Building general-purpose dexterous manipulation policies requires benchmarks that go beyond isolated tasks to systematically evaluate policies across diverse interaction modes, sensory conditions, and robot embodiments. However, existing benchmarks remain limited in task and data diversity, embodiment coverage, or controllable visual variation, hindering studies of cross-task and cross-embodiment generalization. We present DexVerse, a large-scale and modular benchmark for dexterous manipulation. DexVerse includes 100 tasks spanning a broad range of manipulation skills, including object grasping and relocation, articulated-object interaction, functional tool use, bimanual coordination, non-prehensile control, contact-rich behaviors, multi-goal execution, and long-horizon multi-stage task completion. It supports 3 robot arms and 6 dexterous hands, and is extensible to new tasks, assets, and embodiments. To evaluate visuomotor generalization, DexVerse provides configurable visual variations in textures, background, lighting, and camera viewpoints. We further provide a VR-based teleoperation interface and 3,180 demonstrations with synchronized proprioceptive, RGB, depth, point-cloud, and state observations. We benchmark representative methods, including Diffusion Policy, DP3, OpenVLA, and $π_{0.5}$, across 19 tasks. Results reveal substantial challenges in task generalization and visuomotor robustness, establishing DexVerse as a promising testbed for general-purpose dexterous manipulation. Project page: https://ycyao216.github.io/DexVerse.site

AI.tldr: DexVerse is introduced as a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate general-purpose dexterous manipulation policies across a broad range of tasks, sensory conditions, and robot embodiments.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for dexterous manipulation lack diversity in tasks, data, and embodiment coverage or fail to allow controlled visual variations, leading to limited insights into cross-task and cross-embodiment generalization.
AI.method: The authors developed DexVerse, a benchmark with 100 diverse tasks, incorporating configurable visual variations, support for multiple robot arms and dexterous hands, extensibility, a VR-based teleoperation interface, large-scale demonstrations, and evaluations of state-of-the-art methods.
AI.result: DexVerse reveals significant challenges in task generalization and visuomotor robustness when benchmarked with methods like Diffusion Policy, DP3, OpenVLA, and $π_{0.5}$.
AI.conclusion: DexVerse is a critical tool for advancing research in general-purpose dexterous manipulation, providing a modular and extensible testbed for evaluation and improvement of existing methods.

title: Best-of-$N$ TTS Evaluation is Confounded by ASR Family Alignment
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Machine Learning for Audio
summary: Best-of-$N$ (BoN) inference improves content consistency in zero-shot text-to-speech by selecting from $N$ candidates with an automatic speech recognition (ASR) verifier. We identify an underexplored evaluation confound: a verifier's apparent quality depends strongly on which ASR family judges it. On LibriSpeech-PC test-clean~\citep{librispeechpc} with F5-TTS~\citep{f5tts}, verifier rankings reverse across Whisper, wav2vec~2.0, and HuBERT evaluators, and same-family verifier-evaluator pairs recover 2-3$\times$ more oracle headroom than cross-family pairs despite near-identical representations (linear CKA $0.978$) -- a pattern consistent with identity- or lineage-level coupling rather than representational overlap. We propose two \textbf{cross-family rank ensembles} (rank-averaging and conjunctive max-rank) that attain the lowest mean WER across three independent evaluators -- $1.61\%$ at $N{=}10$ ($-12\%$ relative to F5-TTS) -- with no measurable degradation under automatic SIM-o/UTMOS metrics; the best single verifier drives WER from $2.06\%$ to $1.72\%$ ($-16.5\%$) under the official F5-TTS evaluator. We recommend cross-evaluator triangulation as default reporting practice.

AI.tldr: This paper explores Best-of-N (BoN) inference for zero-shot text-to-speech via ASR verifiers and addresses evaluation inconsistencies caused by verifier and evaluator family coupling.
AI.motivation: To investigate and address inconsistencies in the evaluation results due to the alignment or coupling between ASR verifiers and evaluators in zero-shot text-to-speech inference.
AI.method: The paper evaluates how performances of ASR verifiers differ depending on evaluator family, and proposes cross-family rank ensemble methods for better, unbiased performance metrics. These include rank-averaging and conjunctive max-rank approaches.
AI.result: The cross-family rank ensemble methods achieve the lowest mean WER across multiple independent evaluators, reducing WER by 12% compared to traditional practices and improving ASR verification consistency.
AI.conclusion: The study identifies a confounding factor in BoN inference evaluation and proposes solutions for robust and fair reporting. Cross-evaluator triangulation is recommended as a default practice in experimental setups.

title: Dive Into the Implicit Biases of Low-rank Vision-language Alignment
summary: Vision-language alignment, the stage that bridges pretrained vision encoders and large language models, is widely treated as a form of pretraining requiring full-parameter updates. We challenge this view and investigate what happens when low-rank adaptation is applied to the LLM during this stage instead. We find that low-rank alignment not only reduces computational costs but also outperforms full-parameter alignment on most benchmarks. To understand this phenomenon, we systematically characterize the implicit biases introduced by low-rank adaptation during alignment. Empirically, we find that low-rank alignment shifts model behavior from hallucinatory to conservative and preserves per-token linear separability of visual features that full-parameter alignment disrupts, a phenomenon we term LS-curse. Geometrically, low rank aligned models exhibit more homogeneous and structurally stable visual representations, maintaining modality-specific knowledge rather than prematurely fusing entity-level semantics. Theoretically, we establish two theorems showing that low-rank alignment induces preferences for parameter subspaces with flat gradients and feature subspaces robust to perturbations, providing a principled explanation for the observed structure-preserving behavior. Extensive experiments cover ablation over 100 alignment configurations, three families of low-rank operators, and various rank, encoder, and other settings.

AI.tldr: The study investigates low-rank adaptation for vision-language alignment, finding that it reduces computational costs and outperforms full-parameter alignment. The paper identifies structural advantages of low-rank methods over traditional approaches.
AI.motivation: To challenge the prevailing view of vision-language alignment requiring full-parameter updates, and explore the computational and structural benefits of using low-rank adaptations instead.
AI.method: Applied low-rank adaptation to large language models during the vision-language alignment stage and conducted empirical and theoretical analysis of its effects, covering extensive benchmarking, ablation studies, and theoretical modeling.
AI.result: Low-rank adaptation enhances model performance by reducing hallucination, preserving linear separability of visual features, and avoiding premature fusion of modality-specific knowledge. The alignment introduces preferences for flatter gradients and robust feature subspaces.
AI.conclusion: Low-rank adaptation is a viable and superior alternative to full-parameter alignment for vision-language alignment, offering both computational efficiency and structural advantages while maintaining modality-specific integrity.

title: A First-Principles Theory of Slow Thinking and Active Perception
comment: Published on 2026/05/11 in Journal of Machine Learning
summary: As part of a series on first-principles modeling of cognitive functions, this paper attempts to provide a mathematical formulation of thinking and perception. It formally derives slow thinking or more generally, active perception, and encompasses the design, training and inference of slow thinking large language models. Our starting point is the lifting and projection of probability distributions on the observable and latent spaces, with the objective of representing complex data distributions by simple function families such as neural networks. A theory called "active lifting" is proposed, based on the sampling of latent sequences and an intrinsic drive to reduce uncertainty with maximum rate. It derives a large design space, containing the slow thinking models in a subspace that we call the static theory. These models are positioned on the representation hierarchy and sampler hierarchy induced by the static theory, and can be upgraded by climbing the two hierarchies. Active lifting further derives an inference process with an internal time axis, and a training objective that resembles minimum-length coding as well as the invention of languages. Thus, it characterizes the agency of perception, including the emergence of the slow thinking formats. Technical by-products of this theory include a three-stage pathway for improving slow thinking models, a unified approach to constructing encoders and generative models for all data modalities, a priori formation of human-like visual representations, and a possible solution to policy collapse.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a mathematical framework for modeling slow thinking and active perception in cognitive functions, integrating design, training, and inference of large language models using "active lifting."
AI.motivation: To establish a first-principles approach for understanding and modeling cognitive functions, such as thinking and perception, that involve agency and uncertainty reduction.
AI.method: The authors introduce "active lifting," which involves probabilistic lifting and projection techniques to simplify complex data distributions into neural network function families. They define hierarchies for representation and sampling and formulate training and inference objectives.
AI.result: The theories developed provide pathways for improving slow thinking models, unify encoder and generative models across data types, offer insights into human-like visual representation formation, and potentially resolve policy collapse.
AI.conclusion: This work characterizes the mechanisms of active perception and slow thinking, offering theoretical and practical advancements in modeling cognitive functions and improving AI systems.

title: Optimal Learning Rate Scaling Depends on Data in Deep Scalar Linear Networks
summary: In this short note we consider the gradient descent dynamics of deep scalar linear networks, $f(x) = \prod_{l=1}^L w_l x$, which enjoy exact time-course solutions for any integer depth. We show that even in this minimal model, the optimal depth-wise learning rate scaling depends on data, whereas data-agnostic scaling rules fail to transfer across depths. Under the data-dependent optimal scaling, the learning dynamics is independent of data and weakly dependent on depth, resulting in a constant linear convergence rate across all depths including infinity. We further show similar data-dependent effects in deep scalar linear networks with residual connections.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the gradient descent dynamics in deep scalar linear networks and demonstrates that optimal learning rate scaling depends on the data.
AI.motivation: To investigate the scaling rules of learning rates in deep scalar linear networks and their dependence on data.
AI.method: The authors derived exact time-course solutions for gradient descent dynamics and analyzed the impact of depth and residual connections on learning rate scaling.
AI.result: Data-dependent optimal learning rate scaling ensures convergence that is independent of data and weakly dependent on depth, achieving constant linear convergence rates across all depths.
AI.conclusion: Data-agnostic scaling rules are insufficient, highlighting the critical role of data-dependent scaling for consistent convergence across network depths.

title: XALPHA: A Memory-Driven AI Quant Researcher for Hypothesis-to-Code Alpha Discovery
summary: Financial markets are noisy, non-stationary, and high-dimensional, making it difficult to discover predictive and robust trading signals. Alpha discovery has evolved from manual factor design to machine learning, evolutionary search, and recent LLM-based frameworks, improving the efficiency of factor generation, search, and evaluation. However, existing methods still mostly automate isolated steps, rather than functioning as end-to-end quant researchers that can absorb external knowledge, close the hypothesis-to-code validation loop, and learn from accumulated discovery feedback. To fill this gap, we introduce XAlpha, a memory-driven AI Quant Researcher for continuous hypothesis-to-code alpha discovery. XAlpha maintains a multi-source research memory system that integrates report-grounded financial knowledge with discovery feedback from prior generations and research cycles. Guided by this memory system, a Macro Brain plans research themes and selects suitable Archetypes; a Micro Brain transforms the planned hypothesis pool into executable factor code and verifies ex-ante tri-alignment among the hypothesis idea, code logic, and financial plausibility; and a Cross Brain consolidates empirical outcomes into generation-level feedback, cycle-level summaries, and archetype-level research cues for future exploration. In this way, XAlpha turns alpha mining from isolated factor generation into a closed-loop research process that continuously reads, hypothesizes, implements, validates, reflects, and evolves. Experiments on CSI300 show that XAlpha achieves stronger overall alpha discovery performance than representative baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper presents XAlpha, an AI-based system for end-to-end quantitative financial research that automates and improves alpha discovery by integrating a memory-driven closed-loop process.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the limitations of existing financial research systems that primarily automate isolated steps of alpha discovery. Current approaches fail to function as comprehensive quant researchers capable of leveraging external knowledge, performing end-to-end analysis, and learning from feedback.
AI.method: XAlpha introduces a multi-source research memory system and a three-brain architecture (Macro Brain, Micro Brain, and Cross Brain) to mimic human-like quant research. This system performs tasks like hypothesis planning, code execution, and factor validation, while utilizing historical feedback for continuous improvement.
AI.result: Experiments on the CSI300 indicate that XAlpha surpasses existing baseline methods in terms of alpha discovery performance, showcasing efficiency and robust research capabilities.
AI.conclusion: XAlpha successfully transforms alpha mining from a fragmented process into a continuous and iterative research pipeline, demonstrating its potential as a robust AI-driven quant researcher for financial markets.

title: Unpaired Joint Distribution Modeling via Multi-Scale Image Representations
summary: This paper studies the problem of learning a joint distribution from marginal observations, which is inherently ill-posed due to the ambiguity of feasible couplings. We propose LUD-MSR, a latent-variable probabilistic framework that models the joint distribution via auxiliary representations and optimizes evidence lower bounds using only marginal data. Under mild assumptions, we establish an upper bound on the distribution approximation error. This analysis reveals a trade-off in representation learning between domain consistency and information preservation. To address this trade-off, we introduce a Multi-Scale image Representation (MSR) mapping that exploits structural similarity at coarse scales while suppressing domain-specific variations. We show that MSR achieves a more favorable balance of this trade-off compared to existing approaches. Experiments on real-world denoising benchmarks, including cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM), demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LUD-MSR, a framework for learning joint distributions from marginal data, and demonstrates its efficacy using multi-scale representations.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the ill-posed problem of learning joint distributions purely from marginal observations, which arises due to ambiguity in feasible couplings.
AI.method: The authors develop LUD-MSR, a latent-variable probabilistic model that optimizes evidence lower bounds using marginal data. They introduce a Multi-Scale image Representation (MSR) to balance domain consistency and information preservation.
AI.result: The proposed method shows favorable performance by balancing representation trade-offs and outperforms existing methods in denoising tasks such as cryo-electron microscopy.
AI.conclusion: LUD-MSR effectively addresses the ill-posed distribution learning problem by leveraging multi-scale representations, achieving superior results in real-world applications.

title: Playing ZendoWorld: Challenging AI Agents on Active Visual Concept Induction
summary: A central challenge in building intelligent systems is enabling agents to jointly perceive complex inputs, form hypotheses about hidden patterns, and design informative experiments to test them. To study this problem, we propose ZendoWorld, a controlled interactive environment in which agents must infer a logical rule about visual game observations, acquire information by proposing new scenes, and refine their hypotheses based on feedback from the game environment. We evaluate several agents spanning pure VLM reasoning, Bayesian particle filtering, dynamic concept discovery, and neuro-symbolic methods. Our main findings are: (1) high accuracy in predicting labels for observed examples does not imply recovery of the underlying rule; (2) perception and induction are distinct bottlenecks for different agent classes; and (3) VLM-based agents propose near-uninformative experiments, failing to actively reduce hypothesis uncertainty. To compare these results, we collect human data on the task, which reveals a gap in inductive reasoning, particularly for more complex rules. Overall, ZENDOWORLD takes an important step toward evaluating intelligent agents and identifies concrete avenues for improvement, particularly in domains like scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ZendoWorld, where agents infer logic rules from visual observations and design experiments to test hypotheses. Human comparisons reveal challenges in inductive reasoning.
AI.motivation: The study seeks to address the challenge of enabling AI systems to jointly perceive, hypothesize, and test experiments in complex environments.
AI.method: Researchers developed ZendoWorld, combining logical inference with scene generation and feedback-based hypothesis refinement, and assessed agent performance using multiple AI techniques.
AI.result: Key findings highlight issues in rule recovery, perception vs induction bottlenecks, and poor experiment design by VLM agents. Human comparisons reveal gaps in AI reasoning for complex rules.
AI.conclusion: ZendoWorld provides a framework for evaluating AI agents' reasoning and experimentation abilities, offering insights for improving inductive reasoning and scientific discovery in intelligent systems.

title: Distributed Sketching on Data Partitions for OLS Regression
comment: This work has been accepted at Statistics&Probability Letters, 2026
summary: This paper studies distributed sketching for ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, an approach that distributes small sketches of a large data set over multiple machines to separately construct OLS estimators and average them. Unlike prior studies that consider sketching on the whole data set, we consider sketching on partitioned subsets to further reduce computational cost. Under the fixed design setting, we characterize the exact excess loss of the averaged OLS estimator. Results show that this loss is comparable to the established loss for sketching on the whole data set when the divergence among subset covariances is small.

AI.tldr: The paper explores distributed sketching for OLS regression, focusing on partitioned subsets to reduce computational costs.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to optimize the efficiency of OLS regression by using distributed sketching on data subsets.
AI.method: They analyze distributed OLS estimators constructed from partitioned data sketches and average the results.
AI.result: They prove that excess loss remains comparable to traditional methods when subset covariance divergence is minimal.
AI.conclusion: Partitioned sketching can achieve similar performance to whole data set sketching with lower computational cost, provided subset covariance divergence is controlled.

title: TMI: Text-to-Image Meets Image-to-Image for Complementary Data Synthesis to Boost Long-Tailed Instance Segmentation
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. The first two authors contributed equally to this work
summary: Large-vocabulary instance segmentation is constrained by long-tailed category distributions and fine-grained inter-class ambiguity. While data synthesis offers a promising alternative, current paradigms have complementary limitations: text-to-image (T2I) methods inherit noisy pseudo-labels and struggle on rare classes, whereas copy-paste methods compromise contextual realism. To address these issues, we propose a hybrid pipeline coupling T2I generation with context-aware image-to-image (I2I) editing. The T2I branch provides broad category and scene diversity, while a teacher-student scheme ensures label reliability by selectively retaining only prompt-specified categories. To strengthen supervision for rare classes, we introduce VRAIN (Verified Rare-class Augmentation via INstructed editing), a novel I2I editor. VRAIN inserts high-confidence instances at semantically appropriate locations within in-the-wild scenes, yielding semantically coherent and visually natural edits that reduce domain gaps and enable targeted augmentation. On the LVIS benchmark, our method surpasses existing baselines, improving overall AP by up to +4.0 points and rare-class AP by up to +9.5 points, while scaling effectively with backbone capacity. Our project page is available at https://seokhunchoi.github.io/TMI

AI.tldr: The paper addresses challenges in large-vocabulary instance segmentation by proposing a hybrid synthesis pipeline combining text-to-image generation with an image-to-image editor. Their method shows significant performance gains on rare classes.
AI.motivation: Current challenges include long-tailed distributions and inter-class ambiguity, with existing synthesis methods being limited in handling rare classes or producing contextually realistic scenes.
AI.method: The authors propose a hybrid pipeline that integrates text-to-image generation for diverse categories with a context-aware image-to-image editing system named VRAIN. VRAIN enhances rare class augmentation by introducing semantically coherent and high-confidence edits.
AI.result: Testing on the LVIS benchmark, the method improves overall AP by up to +4.0 points and rare-class AP by up to +9.5 points, demonstrating scalability with stronger backbones.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid pipeline offers a robust solution for large-vocabulary instance segmentation challenges, excelling particularly in rare-class detection and improving overall performance via reliable data augmentation.

title: AutoPersonas: A Multi-Timescale Loop Engine for Open-Ended Persona Evolution
comment: 52 pages, 13 figures/tables, ancillary public-safe evaluation artifacts included
summary: Long-term persona agents must remain identifiable while adapting to new events, relationships, evidence, and social conditions. We identify self-locking as a runtime failure mode in continuing persona-life loops: locally plausible events keep appearing while the generated life collapses toward familiar environments, weak relationships, suspended decisions, and stale life stages. We trace this failure to model-level convergence toward high-probability behavioral channels and system-level context gravity from State, memory, history, and environment summaries. We introduce AutoPersonas, a multi-timescale life-environment engine for bounded persona-level recursive self-evolution. It separates environment-side Occurrences, accumulated Observations, and persona State. Its OSO loop admits divergent future-facing material while requiring evidence-governed absorption before State or reachability changes. A three-year compressed simulation exposed environment watermark shells, occurrence-hardening gaps, slow-change accumulation failures, recursive indecision, and weak relationship persistence. An eight-model 40-day stress test generated 1,600 events and found mean rolling 5-day action-category repetition of 95.2%-97.6%, with all models crossing 90% by day 11. Semantic re-keeping found 79.0%-88.0% macro-theme repetition across all direct-loop runs. In a same-runtime 40-day A/B, context-slice masking plus per-sample divergence targeting reduced macro-theme repetition from 61.8% to 36.3% and roughly doubled cumulative theme count. A juvenile-goblin fictional-world run reproduced the anti-fixation regime without hard real-world intrusions. These results support a bounded claim: separating controlled divergence from evidence-governed absorption can reduce persona-environment self-locking while preserving identity continuity.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of maintaining persistent, adaptive personas in long-term simulations by identifying and addressing a failure mode called "self-locking" using a novel system, AutoPersonas.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to solve the problem of creating long-term persona agents that remain coherent and adaptive to new circumstances, without collapsing into repetitive patterns or stagnant life stages.
AI.method: The authors introduced AutoPersonas, a life-environment engine that decouples environment events, observations, and persona states to allow divergent future events while requiring evidence-based updates for state and context.
AI.result: Experimental simulations revealed issues like repetitive behaviors and theme stagnation. However, interventions like context-slice masking and divergence targeting reduced repetition and increased thematic variety, illustrating the effectiveness of the proposed system.
AI.conclusion: AutoPersonas demonstrates that separating divergent event generation from evidence-based state updates can reduce the risk of personas falling into repetitive or stagnant patterns while maintaining their identity over time.

title: Multimodal Unlearning Across Vision, Language, Video, and Audio: Survey of Methods, Datasets, and Benchmarks
summary: With the growing adoption of VLMs, DMs, LLMs, and AFMs, these multimodal foundation models can inadvertently encode sensitive, copyrighted, biased, or unsafe cross-modal associations that originate from their training data. Retraining after deletion requests or policy updates is often impractical, and targeted forgetting remains difficult because knowledge is distributed across shared representations. Multimodal unlearning addresses this challenge by enabling selective removal across modalities while retaining overall utility. This survey offers a unified, system-oriented view of multimodal unlearning across vision, language, audio, and video, grounded in recent advances, emerging applications, and open problems. Our taxonomy enables systematic comparison across model architectures and modalities, clarifying trade-offs among deletion strength, retention, efficiency, reversibility, and robustness. This survey highlights open problems and practical considerations to support future research and deployment of multimodal unlearning. We release a curated repository: https://smsnobin77.github.io/Awesome-Multimodal-Unlearning/

AI.tldr: This survey explores multimodal unlearning, focusing on selective removal of specific information from multimodal foundation models while preserving their utility, addressing issues like sensitive data and unintentional biases.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address challenges posed by foundational multimodal models encoding sensitive or biased associations due to training data and the infeasibility of retraining after deletions or updates.
AI.method: The survey introduces a taxonomy for comparing model architectures and modalities, analyzing trade-offs like deletion strength, retention, efficiency, reversibility, and robustness.
AI.result: They provide a unified view of multimodal unlearning, highlight challenges, and offer practical insights into its deployment to foster future research. Additionally, they release a curated repository.
AI.conclusion: This study calls for increased focus on multimodal unlearning to tackle sensitive and biased data handling issues while ensuring models remain overall practical and efficient.

title: Grounded Event Extraction from SEC 8-K Filings with a Fine-Grained Taxonomy
comment: 9 pages, 8 figures, 1 table. Full dataset and taxonomy available at https://massive.com/docs/rest/stocks/filings/8-k-disclosures?utm_source=research&utm_campaign=8k_tags
summary: Form 8-K filings are the primary channel through which U.S. public companies disclose material events, but the SEC item codes attached to them are coarse: a single item spans routine administrative changes and chief executive departures, and many of the most market-moving disclosures fall into a catch-all item. Large language models make fine-grained labelling feasible at corpus scale, but only if the labels can be traced to the source text and shown to be reliable. We present a two-stage system that tags 8-K disclosures against a three-tier taxonomy of 119 event types. The first stage constrains output to valid taxonomy entries and anchors every tag to a verbatim quote via fuzzy n-gram validation; the second re-grades each cited quote against the category definition to produce a quality score. Applying the system to 292,984 filings from 2022 to 2026 yields 601,088 grounded event tags, which we release. Over 5,125 stratified tags, an LLM judge finds precision rises monotonically with the quality score, from 12% to 96%, while unsupported tags fall from 8% to near zero. Ablation shows the score is calibrated only when assigned in a dedicated second pass. An event study on unsigned abnormal returns confirms, without any language model, that the taxonomy separates economically distinct events sharing an item code.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a two-stage system for fine-grained labeling of SEC Form 8-K filings, achieving accurate categorization of event types with reliable text anchoring using modern language models.
AI.motivation: To address the inadequacy of coarse SEC item codes in capturing the diversity and granularity of material events disclosed in Form 8-K filings, and to improve both label specificity and data reliability at scale, using large language models.
AI.method: The authors propose a system comprising two stages: the first ensures valid output anchored to verbatim quotes via fuzzy n-gram validation, while the second re-evaluates and scores tags based on alignment with category definitions for reliability.
AI.result: The system analyzed 292,984 filings (2022-2026) and generated 601,088 grounded event tags, demonstrating improved precision with quality scores ranging from 12% to 96%, and minimizing unsupported tags to near-zero levels.
AI.conclusion: The taxonomy and labeling system are effective at distinguishing economically distinct events, proving their usefulness over current coarse item codes, and demonstrating success in incorporating language models for regulatory filings.

title: Metrics or Mirage? An Audit of Evaluation Inconsistencies in Colonoscopy Polyp Segmentation Benchmarks
summary: Progress in colonoscopy polyp segmentation is routinely reported through leaderboard comparisons on a small set of public benchmarks. We argue that this apparent progress is difficult to verify: a systematic audit of \textbf{27 papers} published between 2015 and 2026 reveals three structural problems in how the community evaluates models. \textbf{First}, 25 of 27 papers \textit{omit the Hausdorff distance}. Hausdorff distance is a boundary-accuracy metric with direct clinical relevance for detecting flat or small polyps, and is a standard in radiotherapy segmentation. \textbf{Second}, at least five \textit{incompatible train/test split protocols} co-exist across papers reporting results on the same two datasets (Kvasir-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB), making published Dice scores non-comparable even when they appear in the same leaderboard column. \textbf{Third}, 26 of 27 papers make \textit{performance claims without any statistical significance test}. Strikingly, four papers published \emph{after} the Metrics Reloaded framework~\cite{metricsreloaded2024} (Maier-Hein et al., \textit{Nature Methods} 2024) perpetuate these same problems, suggesting that general-purpose metric guidance has not yet reached the colonoscopy sub-community. To show these problems are not merely cosmetic, we re-evaluate five representative models under three controlled protocols with a single uniform scorer, and find that the reported metric conceals large boundary and recall failures, that the ``best'' model changes with the metric, and that near-tied rankings reverse across random splits. We propose a five-point \textbf{Polyp Segmentation Reporting Checklist}~(PSRC) as a lightweight, domain-adapted corrective.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques the progress in colonoscopy polyp segmentation evaluation, finding significant methodological flaws in how models are assessed.
AI.motivation: To scrutinize the validity and reproducibility of reported progress in colonoscopy polyp segmentation, given issues such as incomplete metrics, inconsistent protocols, and lack of statistical rigor.
AI.method: A systematic audit of 27 relevant papers was performed, followed by controlled re-evaluation of five models with standardized protocols and scoring.
AI.result: The audit revealed issues with metric omissions (e.g., Hausdorff distance), inconsistent train/test splits, and a lack of statistical validity in performance claims. Re-evaluation exposed concealed weaknesses and inconsistencies in ranking models.
AI.conclusion: The authors propose a five-point Polyp Segmentation Reporting Checklist (PSRC) to address these prevalent evaluation shortcomings and improve transparency in the field.

title: Compete Then Collaborate: Frontier AI Teachers Build a Verifiable Curriculum to Improve a Coding Student Beyond Imitation
summary: Large language models increasingly serve as teachers generating training data for smaller students. Prior multi-teacher knowledge distillation methods merge outputs without determining which frontier model teaches best, often relying on an LLM judge biased toward its own outputs. We introduce a compete-then-collaborate framework where four frontier AI teachers (Claude, Codex-GPT, Grok, Gemini) are ranked head-to-head by an execution-based judge (unit tests and stdin-stdout checks) with fairness controls, and then collaborate to build a verifiable curriculum for a student (Qwen2.5-Coder). We report three findings. (1) Under execution verification, all teachers solve standard problems near-perfectly after self-correction (99-100%) due to a saturation effect, but harder competition problems separate them (Gemini 77% > Claude 69% = Codex 69% > Grok 50%); however, the robust student-side results do not depend on teacher ranking. (2) Imitation (SFT) on verified solutions does not improve, and can degrade, an already-competent student at 7B and 32B (e.g., from 76.7% to 72.7% on MBPP-test, and 5.9% to 2.9% on competition problems). (3) Using the same collaborative curriculum as a reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) environment improves the student (from 5.9% to 8.8% peak on competition problems, a +49% relative gain), reversing SFT's direction. The value of AI-teacher collaboration lies not in pooling answers to imitate, but in jointly constructing a verifiable environment where the student learns by doing. We release a reproducible on-prem pipeline (NVIDIA GB10) with framework patches for running GRPO on a bleeding-edge stack.

AI.tldr: The study introduces a "compete-then-collaborate" AI-teaching framework, where four frontier AI models are evaluated fairly and collaborate to generate a curriculum for a smaller student model. Results show imitation learning degrades student performance, but reinforcement learning in the constructed environment improves outcomes significantly.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address biases and ineffectiveness in multi-teacher knowledge distillation where LLM-generated training data often lacks proper fairness controls, and imitation methods fail to improve student performance.
AI.method: The method uses a compete-then-collaborate approach: four AI frontier models undergo fair head-to-head evaluation based on an execution-based judge. After ranking, they collaboratively construct a verifiable learning environment for the student model. Outcomes from imitation and reinforcement learning are compared.
AI.result: 1) All teacher models perform near-perfectly under standard conditions, but harder problems reveal differences in skills. Robust student results are independent of teacher rankings. 2) Imitation learning degrades the performance of students. 3) Reinforcement learning with a collaborative curriculum significantly boosts student performance on difficult problems.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that collaborative AI teaching should prioritize creating verifiable learning environments for students instead of just pooling answers for imitation learning. Reinforcement learning proves more effective for enabling a student model's growth through learning by doing.

title: Efficient Safety Alignment of Language Models via Latent Personality Traits
summary: Current safety methods for large language models are known to be vulnerable to adversarial attacks, motivating research into robust alternatives. Latent Adversarial Training (LAT) is among the most effective defenses, but can degrade utility and requires training on large datasets of harmful prompts. We introduce Latent Personality Alignment (LPA), which replaces explicit harm refusal with adversarial training on just 66 harm-agnostic statements drawn from psychometric personality literature. We hypothesize that personality-anchored representations share latent structure with harm avoidance, so adversarially stabilizing them implicitly constrains the subspace exploited by jailbreak attacks. LPA achieves near-zero attack success rates on HarmBench across direct requests and five jailbreak methods, despite never seeing harmful content during training and no loss of performance on standard benchmarks. Moreover, the training process is lightweight; the entire procedure completes in minutes on a single GPU and uses 75x fewer examples than standard LAT. Extensive ablations demonstrate the robustness, efficiency, and generalization of our method.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Latent Personality Alignment (LPA), an efficient alternative to Latent Adversarial Training (LAT) for improving the robustness of language models against adversarial attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing safety methods for language models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks and often require large datasets of harmful prompts for training. This motivates the need for more robust and efficient alternatives.
AI.method: The authors propose Latent Personality Alignment (LPA), which employs adversarial training on 66 harm-agnostic personality-based statements instead of harmful content. This approach stabilizes personality-anchored latent representations to implicitly reduce susceptibility to adversarial attacks.
AI.result: LPA achieves near-zero attack success rates on HarmBench, with no degradation in standard task performance, and uses significantly less data and computational resources compared to LAT.
AI.conclusion: LPA is a lightweight, effective, and generalizable method for enhancing the robustness of language models against adversarial attacks without requiring large datasets of harmful prompts.

title: Echoes Across Vietnam's Highlands, Delta, and Coast: A Multilingual Corpus for Cham, Khmer, and Tay-Nung
summary: Vietnam's ethnic minority languages are almost absent from the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP), and the challenge goes beyond data scarcity: Cham, Khmer, and Tay-Nung differ sharply in script, Vietnamese contact, and standardization, conditions under which standard multilingual adaptation can learn the wrong signals. We introduce CKTN, the first corpus and benchmark for these languages (44,367 documents, 24M subword tokens), spanning continued pretraining, category classification, and summary-document retrieval. We show that existing multilingual encoders severely fragment these languages, and that common adaptation metrics can mislead: models may lower language-modeling loss or excel at lexical-overlap retrieval while still failing at semantic generalization across documents. We address this with a script-aware adaptation recipe - vocabulary augmentation combined with calibrated replaced-token pretraining - that prevents the discriminator from exploiting trivial script mismatches. The result is an encoder with substantially less fragmentation and the strongest classification performance among evaluated models, exposing the limits of lexical-overlap retrieval as an evaluation signal.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CKTN, the first corpus and benchmark for Vietnam's underrepresented minority languages in NLP. It identifies key challenges in multilingual NLP for these languages and proposes a script-aware model adaptation technique to address them.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of resources and specific challenges in NLP for Vietnam's minority languages (Cham, Khmer, Tay-Nung) and improve performance in tasks requiring semantic generalization.
AI.method: They developed CKTN, a corpus with over 44,000 documents, focusing on designing a vocabulary augmentation and script-aware adaptation method with calibrated replaced-token pretraining to address language fragmentation issues.
AI.result: Their approach reduced language fragmentation and achieved the strongest classification performance among the evaluated models, while also exposing limitations of traditional evaluation methods relying on lexical overlap.
AI.conclusion: The proposed script-aware adaptation significantly improves NLP for Vietnam's minority languages, highlighting the need to rethink traditional evaluation metrics and methodologies in multilingual NLP.

title: Benchmark Evaluation of Feredated Learning on Multi-organ Images
summary: The privacy requirements of medical data and its substantial variations across organs and modalities hinder the clinical implementation of medical AI. Federated learning (FL) is a feasible approach to overcome these challenges. Due to the continuous emergence of FL algorithms and the highly heterogeneous nature of medical data, objectively evaluating their performance in real-world clinical settings remains difficult. Therefore, a comprehensive federated medical imaging benchmark, serving as a unified evaluation standard, is crucial for advancing the technology toward reliable clinical application. Existing federated medical imaging benchmarks have not yet adequately incorporated state-of-the-art algorithms, are limited to data from single organs or modalities, and overly emphasize model accuracy, making it difficult to comprehensively assess the overall efficacy of FL in real-world medical environments. To address these challenges, we developed the MobenFL benchmark. This benchmark integrates 20 cutting-edge FL algorithms and 22 medical imaging datasets, covering 12 critical organs across the human body, surpassing existing benchmark in breadth. In terms of evaluation dimensions, MobenFL not only assesses performance but also systematically incorporates key metrics such as algorithmic efficiency and privacy protection capabilities. Additionally, it conducts specialized evaluations for complex real-world clinical scenarios involving different diseases, devices, and imaging modalities, thereby providing a comprehensive and in-depth evaluation framework for the clinical application of FL in the medical field.

AI.tldr: MobenFL is a federated medical imaging benchmark that integrates diverse datasets and algorithms, offering a comprehensive evaluation framework for clinical AI applications.
AI.motivation: The need for a streamlined evaluation framework arises from privacy concerns, variations in medical data, and challenges posed by assessing federated learning (FL) in real-world medical environments.
AI.method: MobenFL includes 20 state-of-the-art FL algorithms and 22 datasets across 12 organs, introducing metrics like efficiency and privacy alongside traditional performance measures.
AI.result: MobenFL provides a far-reaching evaluation standard addressing real-world complexities of different diseases, devices, and imaging modalities, outperforming existing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: This framework advances FL evaluation, promoting its credible clinical application by addressing both performance and practical challenges in medical imaging.

title: MentalHospital: A Virtual Environment for Evaluating Psychiatric Clinical Encounters
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance on isolated psychiatric tasks, including dialogue, diagnosis, and treatment planning, yet existing benchmarks rarely simulate complete psychiatric clinical encounters. We introduce $\textbf{MentalHospital}$, a virtual evaluation environment for LLM-based psychiatric clinical encounters. MentalHospital instantiates the Subjective Interviewing, Objective Examination, Diagnostic Assessment, and Treatment Planning (S.O.A.P.) workflow, using skill-augmented standardized patients constructed from 1,193 de-identified psychiatric electronic health record (EHR) cases spanning all major ICD-11 categories and 76 disorders. Each encounter is assessed through a dual-track protocol that combines objective comparison against EHR-derived references with subjective assessment of clinical process quality. To scale specialist judgment, we develop $\textbf{MentalEval}$, five domain-specific evaluators covering communication empathy, interviewing professionalism, clinical-note quality, diagnostic rigor, and treatment appropriateness, trained with rubric-grounded SFT and expert-guided DPO. Survey responses from 22 clinicians support MentalHospital's clinical fidelity (3.88/5), while MentalEval achieves strong expert alignment with an average QWK of 0.944. Benchmarking shows that even the strongest LLM trails clinicians by 37.28 percentage points in objective psychiatric competence, with mental status assessment as a key bottleneck.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MentalHospital, an evaluation environment for testing language models on simulated psychiatric clinical encounters, and MentalEval, domain-specific evaluators for assessing clinical competency.
AI.motivation: While large language models have shown promising performance on isolated psychiatric tasks, there is a lack of benchmarks simulating comprehensive psychiatric clinical encounters.
AI.method: The authors developed MentalHospital, which uses a SOAP-based workflow with standardized patients created from 1,193 psychiatric EHR cases and assessed through both objective metrics and subjective evaluations. They also created MentalEval, specialized evaluators trained with expert guidance to measure various clinical dimensions.
AI.result: MentalEval demonstrates strong expert alignment, with a QWK of 0.944. Benchmarking results show that even the best LLMs significantly underperform clinicians in psychiatric competence, especially in mental status assessments.
AI.conclusion: Comprehensive evaluation environments like MentalHospital and specialized evaluators like MentalEval are essential for measuring LLMs' capabilities in psychiatric clinical encounters, revealing the current gaps and limitations compared to human clinicians.

title: path_boost: A Python Package for Interpretable Graph-Level Prediction using Path-Based Gradient Boosting
comment: 27 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables. Code available at https://github.com/Claudio-Me/extended_path_boost and on PyPI (path-boost)
summary: We present path_boost, a Python package for interpretable supervised learning on graph-structured input data. The package implements PathBoost, a gradient boosting algorithm that automatically discovers predictive labeled paths within graphs during the learning process. Unlike graph neural networks, which are generally difficult to interpret, PathBoost produces an additive prediction model over path-based features that explicitly reveals which substructures drive predictions. To avoid an exhaustive enumeration of all possible paths, the algorithm iteratively selects and extends paths during learning based on their predictive power, using boosting to combine weak learners into a strong ensemble. The package supports both regression and binary classification. Key features include compatibility with scikit-learn workflows, support for custom base learners and selectors, automatic starting node selection, parallel training across anchor nodes, and built-in variable importance computation. We demonstrate PathBoost on molecular property prediction of transition metal compounds, where atoms serve as nodes and bonds as edges, and further benchmark PathBoost against an established graph neural network and a graph kernel method across six molecular datasets. The package is available on PyPI and GitHub under an open-source license.

AI.tldr: "path_boost" is a Python tool for interpretable supervised learning on graph data, using labeled path-based features instead of black-box graph neural networks.
AI.motivation: To provide a more interpretable method for supervised learning on graph data compared to graph neural networks (which are often opaque).
AI.method: Implements a gradient boosting algorithm (PathBoost) that discovers predictive paths in graphs by selecting, extending, and combining path-based weak learners into a strong model during training.
AI.result: The PathBoost package successfully performs regression and binary classification, and is demonstrated to predict molecular properties better than or comparably to graph neural networks and graph kernel methods on six datasets.
AI.conclusion: PathBoost enables interpretable predictions on graph-structured data, with features such as scikit-learn compatibility, customizability, parallel training, and variable importance computation.

title: Large-Language-Models-as-a-Judge in Theory-Agnostic Adaptive Metric-Alignment for Prototypical Networks in Personality Recognition
summary: Personality recognition has traditionally been constrained by theory-dependent formulations, where models are trained to fit predefined psychological taxonomies rather than uncovering shared underlying behavioral structure. This limits generalization, as personality itself is better understood as theory-invariant, while existing annotations reflect only partial and sometimes inconsistent views of the same latent traits. In this work, we introduce JAM ((J)udge for (A)daptive (M)etric-Alignment), a theory-agnostic framework that shifts learning from adapting to predefined personality theories toward discovering unified latent pseudo-facets that capture shared psychological structure. Rather than constraining the model to any personality taxonomy during training or inference, the framework learns generalizable psychological representations and can infer an individual's latent psychological profile directly from the textual samples, without requiring theory-specific labels. JAM achieves this through an Attention-Pooled Graph Prototypical Network that learns structured representations via clustering in embedding space, together with a Cross-Theory Harmonization (CTH) approach that integrates (i) Human-Guided Linkage and (ii) Machine-Induced Consensus to unify heterogeneous datasets without relying on predefined labels. To further improve robustness and data quality, we incorporate an LLM-as-a-Judge mechanism operating in two configurations, (i) LLM-before-the-loop and (ii) LLM-in-the-loop which identifies ambiguous samples to guide adaptive metric learning. Experiments show that JAM improves cross-framework generalization and performance, establishing a strong step toward theory-agnostic personality inference and supporting low-resource personality theories. The related code repository, model weights, and artifacts are available at https://research.jingjietan.com/JAM

AI.tldr: The paper introduces JAM, a framework for personality recognition bypassing traditional psychological taxonomy constraints, using advanced techniques like graph-based learning and LLM incorporation for better generalization.
AI.motivation: Current personality recognition models rely on theory-specific taxonomies, limiting their generalization as these taxonomies are incomplete and inconsistent representations of underlying traits.
AI.method: The JAM framework employs an Attention-Pooled Graph Prototypical Network and Cross-Theory Harmonization (CTH) to unify datasets while integrating LLM mechanisms for adaptive metric learning and robustness.
AI.result: JAM demonstrates improved cross-framework generalization and supports low-resource personality theories.
AI.conclusion: JAM marks significant progress in theory-agnostic personality inference and offers tools to discover shared psychological structures across diverse datasets.

title: LUMI: Tokenizer-Agnostic LLM-Based Lossless Image Compression
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based lossless image compression methods typically represent pixel data through the native text interface of a pretrained model, converting pixel values into token sequences that the LLM processes through its vocabulary head. This design shows that pretrained language models can provide probability estimates for image coding, but it also couples compression to tokenizer behavior, vocabulary-specific numeric tokens, and model-family-specific adaptation. In this paper, we present LUMI (LLM-based Unified Model-agnostic lossless Image compression), a tokenizer-agnostic framework for lossless RGB image compression with frozen LLM backbones. LUMI replaces pixel-as-text tokenization with a pixel embedding module that maps raw intensity and channel information into the continuous embedding space of the LLM. It further introduces intra-patch position encoding to retain two-dimensional spatial structure after flattening, and uses a 256-way prediction head to produce probabilities over the native pixel alphabet. Only the pixel embedding, position encoding, soft-prefix parameters, and prediction head are trained, while the LLM backbone remains fixed. Experiments on natural, medical, and remote-sensing image benchmarks with LLaMA, Qwen, and Gemma backbones show that LUMI provides a unified interface across tokenizer families, achieves competitive compression rates, and improves cross-domain robustness over tokenizer-based LLM compression baselines. These results formulate LLM-based lossless image compression as pixel-space adaptation of frozen foundation models rather than tokenizer-specific language-symbol modeling.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes LUMI, a framework for lossless image compression using frozen large language model (LLM) backbones, achieving competitive performance with improved robustness across domains.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based image compression methods depend on tokenizers and are tied to specific model-family adaptations, limiting their robustness and applicability. The motivation is to create a unified, tokenizer-agnostic framework for lossless image compression using frozen LLMs.
AI.method: The proposed method, LUMI, uses a pixel embedding module to map raw image data into an LLM's embedding space, incorporates intra-patch position encoding to retain spatial structure, and uses a separate 256-way prediction head for pixel probability estimation. Only parts of the framework, excluding the LLM backbone, are trained.
AI.result: LUMI achieves competitive compression rates and greater cross-domain robustness compared to tokenizer-based LLM compression baselines on various natural, medical, and remote-sensing image benchmarks with different LLM backbones.
AI.conclusion: LUMI demonstrates that lossless image compression can be achieved by adapting pixel-space representations with frozen foundation models, paving the way for a unified, tokenizer-agnostic image compression framework.

title: Different Teachers, Different Capabilities: Sub-1B On-Device Distillation for Structured Text Enrichment
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures. has a same-size non-reasoning-teacher control, a three-judge LLM-as-a-judge panel with a negative control, full-source faithfulness grading, and a per-field routing analysis
summary: High-volume structured extraction pays a large model's latency on every item, so distilling the task into a small on-device model is attractive: comparable output at a fraction of the time and cost. We measure what that distillation actually delivers, per sub-task. Each news article is mapped to one JSON object with a short summary and five categorical labels. We distill an 8B reasoning teacher (deepseek-r1:8b) into a 0.6B student (Qwen3-0.6B; QLoRA, three seeds), and add two teacher controls: a same-size non-reasoning teacher and a larger managed pipeline. A blinded, reference-free, three-judge panel scores every arm against the full article, alongside two non-distillation baselines, few-shot prompting and constrained decoding. The student runs at about 0.8 s per article against the teacher's 39 s, and recovers 58% of the base-to-teacher gap on summary quality, beating its primary baseline (constrained decoding) by +16.8 points and few-shot prompting by a secondary +4.9. A same-size non-reasoning teacher trains a student no better than the untuned base, so the summary gain follows from the teacher's reasoning nature rather than its scale. Capabilities then split by teacher: the reasoning teacher transfers writing quality and the managed pipeline transfers label diversity, while a same-size instruction teacher's students stay more grounded on the 22 short, thin-source articles in the 93-item test set (74 versus 55 faithful), where the reasoning-lineage student fabricates. That grounding difference is a consistent ordering rather than a significant aggregate effect, and the subgroup is small, so we report it as a direction. Because no single engine wins every field, the deliverable is a per-field routing map for on-device enrichment.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the distillation of a large model into smaller on-device models for structured data extraction tasks, demonstrating notable gains in efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to make high-volume structured extraction tasks faster and more cost-effective by distilling large models into smaller, on-device models, without significantly compromising on model quality.
AI.method: The authors distill an 8B reasoning teacher model into a smaller 0.6B student model using QLoRA with three seeds. The study includes teacher controls and evaluates the distillation performance using blinded evaluations against multiple baselines such as few-shot prompting and constrained decoding.
AI.result: The distilled student model achieves faster processing times (0.8 seconds vs. 39 seconds per article) while recovering 58% of the quality gap between the base model and the larger teacher. It outperformed baselines like constrained decoding (+16.8 points) and few-shot prompting (+4.9 points). Capabilities and quality vary depending on teacher characteristics.
AI.conclusion: Model distillation offers significant efficiencies in structured extraction tasks, with trade-offs in transferred capabilities. No single model excels at all metrics, warranting a specialized, per-field routing approach for on-device applications.

title: Evaluating the Generalizability of Foundation Models for Extreme Environmental Events: Case Study of California Wildfire PM2.5
summary: Wildfire smoke events produce extreme PM$_{2.5}$ concentrations that pose severe public health risks, yet forecasting rare, hazardous-level spikes remains a fundamental challenge. Time series foundation models (TSFMs), pretrained models offering zero-shot inference and efficient adaptation, perform strongly on general benchmarks, but their behavior under extreme out-of-distribution conditions is poorly understood. We present the first systematic benchmark comparing six TSFM configurations (zero-shot TimesFM, Chronos-2, Moirai-2, and Time-MoE, plus LoRA fine-tuned Chronos-2 and Time-MoE) against fully-trained baselines (LSTM, BiLSTM, Transformer) and naive persistence on a 12-year (2013--2025) hourly PM$_{2.5}$ dataset covering 1,375 wildfire incidents across 79 California monitoring sites. A leave-one-incident-out (LOIO) protocol evaluates generalization to unseen fires, using MAE, RMSE, and exceedance F1 at EPA AQI thresholds across 6-, 12-, and 24-hour horizons. Results reveal a consistent hierarchy. The BiLSTM achieves the lowest MAE ($5.16\,μg/m^3$) and the highest exceedance F1 at every threshold, including the Hazardous band ($>225.5\,μg/m^3$), reaching 0.63 versus at most 0.54 for any foundation model. Zero-shot TSFMs improve on persistence only modestly, and zero-shot Chronos-2 exhibits severe RMSE tail instability ($23.4\,μg/m^3$, negative $R^2$) from sporadic large errors. LoRA fine-tuning substantially improves both adapted families and largely repairs this instability, yet no foundation model surpasses the trained recurrent baselines on any metric. These findings challenge the assumption that larger pretrained models universally dominate environmental forecasting and provide actionable deployment guidance for wildfire air quality prediction.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates time series foundation models (TSFMs) for forecasting wildfire-related PM$_{2.5}$ air quality extremes, finding traditional baselines outperform TSFMs in accuracy and stability.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of forecasting hazardous-level PM$_{2.5}$ spikes caused by wildfire smoke, which pose significant public health risks, and to assess whether TSFMs can perform better under extreme conditions.
AI.method: Compared six TSFM configurations (including zero-shot and fine-tuned) against fully-trained baselines (LSTM, BiLSTM, Transformer) and naive persistence using a 12-year dataset and a leave-one-incident-out (LOIO) evaluation protocol.
AI.result: The BiLSTM outperformed all models, achieving the lowest MAE (5.16µg/m³) and highest exceedance F1 scores. Zero-shot TSFMs showed minimal improvement over naive persistence and were unstable, though fine-tuning improved reliability.
AI.conclusion: While TSFMs have potential, traditional trained baselines such as BiLSTM are currently superior for wildfire air quality forecasting, challenging the dominance of pretrained models in this specific domain.

title: Input-Constrained Spatiotemporal Tubes for Safe Navigation of Unknown Euler-Lagrange Systems in Dynamic Environments
summary: Safe navigation in dynamic environments is challenging when system dynamics are unknown and actuator inputs are limited. Existing methods either rely on accurate models, require online optimization, or do not explicitly account for input constraints. This paper presents a real-time control framework for unknown Euler-Lagrange systems that guarantees finite-time reach-avoid-stay (FT-RAS) specifications while respecting actuator limits. We extend the spatiotemporal tube (STT) framework by incorporating input constraints into the controller design and derive offline-verifiable feasibility conditions that relate the available control authority to the tube design and uncertainty bounds. The resulting framework is approximation-free and computationally efficient, making it suitable for real-time implementation. The proposed approach is validated through simulations on a mobile robot, a quadrotor, and a spacecraft, together with hardware experiments on a mobile robot, demonstrating safe navigation while satisfying actuator constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a real-time control framework for ensuring safe navigation in unknown dynamic systems with actuator input limits.
AI.motivation: Addressing challenges in safe navigation for systems with unknown dynamics and actuator limitations.
AI.method: Extends the spatiotemporal tube (STT) framework to include input constraints and designs offline-verifiable feasibility conditions.
AI.result: Validated through simulations on mobile robots, quadrotors, and spacecraft, along with hardware experiments on a mobile robot.
AI.conclusion: The framework guarantees finite-time reach-avoid-stay specifications, operates computationally efficiently, and is suitable for real-time applications.

title: Prompt Compression via Activation Aggregation
summary: Large language models process prompts by propagating activations through dozens of layers before generating a response. We ask whether the task-relevant information contained in an instruction prompt can be compressed into a single activation vector and re-injected into the model, replacing the original token sequence? We show this is achievable using a learned weighted sum of activations extracted at an intermediate layer and injected at an early layer of the target LLM. The compressed vector preserves task-relevant information, incurring an accuracy drop of under $2\%$ relative to full prompt processing. Beyond its practical implications, including reducing per-query computation for fixed instruction prompts without reprocessing the original token sequence, our analysis reveals structure in the activation space of LLMs: (i) mid-layer representations transfer meaningfully to early layers, suggesting a degree of cross-layer compatibility in how information is encoded; (ii) a single activation vector encodes a quantifiable and recoverable amount of semantic information; (iii) a weighted sum of activations is a robust representation compressor.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes compressing instruction prompts into a single activation vector for large language models, showing minimal accuracy loss during task performance.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to enhance efficiency in large language model processing by investigating whether task-relevant information in prompts can be compacted into a single activation vector.
AI.method: A learned weighted sum of activations is extracted from an intermediate layer and re-injected into an early layer of the model, replacing the token sequence.
AI.result: The compressed vector maintained accuracy with less than a 2% drop compared to standard prompt processing, reducing computational demands significantly.
AI.conclusion: This approach reveals cross-layer compatibility in activation spaces, evidences robust semantic compression, and offers practical benefits for computational efficiency.

title: Multimodal 3D LUT Generation via StatLUT with Statistical Features for Photorealistic Style Transfer
summary: Photorealistic Style Transfer (PST) aims to transfer the color and tonal style of a reference to a content image while strictly preserving its structural integrity. However, existing deep learning-based methods inherently suffer from semantic entanglement caused by pre-trained image encoders, leading to unnatural spatial distortions. Moreover, current pixel-level mapping paradigms often ignore color gamut topology, resulting in color banding, while also lacking the multimodal capability for intuitive text-driven control. To address these bottlenecks, we propose StatLUT, an innovative multimodal framework for 3D LUT generation. First, we bypass traditional encoders and introduce a Lab-Extractor to derive spatially-agnostic statistical features, fundamentally decoupling color distributions from structural semantics to ensure artifact-free rendering. Second, we formulate LUT generation as a Transformer-based Seq2Seq translation task, utilizing a Multi-dimensional Residual Mapper (MR-Mapper) to predict topologically smooth 3D LUTs. Finally, to break the single-modal barrier, we propose the H-Diffuser, a lightweight Diffusion Transformer that directly synthesizes statistical features from natural language prompts, enabling flexible text-driven color grading. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that StatLUT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both visual quality and quantitative metrics, pioneering a highly robust and flexible paradigm for multimodal photorealistic style transfer.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new framework, StatLUT, for photorealistic style transfer using 3D LUTs that ensures structural fidelity and offers multimodal input support.
AI.motivation: Current photorealistic style transfer methods face issues like semantic entanglement and unnatural distortions due to image encoders, as well as color banding and lack of text-based control.
AI.method: StatLUT introduces three key components: (1) a Lab-Extractor for spatially-agnostic features to avoid structural distortions, (2) a Transformer-based MR-Mapper for smooth 3D LUT generation, and (3) an H-Diffuser for text-driven control via statistical feature synthesis.
AI.result: The proposed StatLUT framework achieves superior performance in visual quality and quantitative metrics, surpassing state-of-the-art methods on benchmark tests.
